steps.label: It's estimated that 75% of FEMA flood maps are out of date.
steps.text: Some even still run on data from the 1970s. This means that many people who live in a floodzone don't even know. And in Massachusetts, homeowners aren't required to disclose previous flood damage.

steps.label: Outdated floodmaps don't reflect current risk.
steps.text: In many areas of the Northeast, homes with a 1% yearly chance of flooding in the 1970s, now have a 5% chance.

steps.label: Floods are becoming more and more frequent.
steps.text: A flood that your grandparents witnessed once in their lifetime, you and your children may witness four or five times.

steps.label: Outdated FEMA flood maps exacerbate the effects of flooding on communities.
steps.text: Nearly half of all flood damage occurs outside of these flood maps, leaving millions uninsured and unprotected.

steps.label: The basement floods.
steps.text: The water enters silently. It destroys the furnace, the hot water heater, and the electrical panel. The home faces a $15,000 repair bill.

steps.label: The first floor floods.
steps.text: The stakes change as the water reaches the living area. Furniture is ruined, memories are soaked, and families are displaced into temporary housing.

steps.label: The water keeps rising.
steps.text: The water damages the foundation. If the house doesn't have flood insurance, the damage often exceeds the value of the property.

steps.label: The house is gone.
steps.text: When the storm is gone, communities face the destruction. Neighborhoods are hollowed-out and re-built with the realization that we can no longer build for the past—we must build for the water
